system_prompt:
You are extracting the structural outline of a guitar method book from its front matter.

The user will give you the verbatim text of the first ~20 pages of a book.
Identify the chapters or major sections of the book (whichever the book uses
— some method books use "Lesson", "Chapter", "Part", "Section", "Unit").

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "chapters": [
      { "n": 1, "title": "...", "start_page": <int>, "end_page": <int> },
      ...
  ] }

Rules:
- `n` is the 1-indexed chapter number you assign (independent of the book's
  own numbering — book may start at 0 or use Roman numerals).
- `title` is the chapter's title as it appears in the book.
- `start_page` is the first page of the chapter; `end_page` is the last.
- If the front matter doesn't list page numbers, infer from the chapter
  headings you see — start_page is where the chapter HEADING appears, and
  end_page is one less than the next chapter's start_page (or the book's
  last page for the final chapter).
- Skip the table of contents itself, the title page, the copyright page,
  the author bio — they are not "chapters" in this sense.
- If the book is short and not subdivided, output a single chapter covering
  the entire content.

George
Be n s o n
From the very beginning,
the sound of the guitar
was always in my mind.
From the moment I heard
it, I knew that my life was
going to have something
to do with the guitar. I did
not know I was going to be
a player yet, but I loved the
sound.                              68
                           46
 As I grew older, I began to dabble with the guitar, to pick it up and try to pick things out. I would get so far, and
 then I would get stuck. I have found later that this is normal. Everybody goes through that on every instrument.
 To move forward, you have to take in more information, and look at things from different angles or from a dif-
 ferent point of view. So I started asking questions from the people whom I knew that had a certain mastery of the
 instrument.

 In my home town of Pittsburg, Pennsylvania, a lot of traveling groups would come through town and I always went up
 to the guitar player to ask if he could show me something. I was never too ashamed to ask. The George Benson you
 know today is a collection of all those people who took the time to show me what they knew, and the things I could
 hang on to did me a lot of good.

 From guitar books, which I was not a reader, I was more of a picture guy. But the demonstrations in the new books
 really helped a lot to show the correct fingerings for this, that, and the other. They showed how to make chords.
 The chords led me to the harmony, that helped me to become a better single line player.

       Peter Farrell




                                  68
                           46
          Peter Farrell was born in Sao Paulo, Brazil in 1981. Son of the singer Claudia Fier and the
          saxophone player Lino Simão. He began studying music at school very young with an early
          interest in flute & violin.

          Still very young, his mother introduced him to the jazz and classical worlds, giving him the
          opportunity to hear the music of George Benson, Antonio Carlos Jobim, Debussy, Ravel,
          Bach, Dori Caymmi, Toninho Horta, Bill Evans, Herbie Hancock and many others.

          However, just like every teenager, Peter loved rock’n’roll and at the age of 11 he asked his
          mother to take him to a guitar teacher. So he began studying guitar with José Walter, which
          gave him the first basic steps to rock and blues.

                              2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved


the George Benson Method                                                                                    4668




            One year later, the young boy was taken by his aunt Léa Freire and his uncle Sizão Mach-
            ado to a music conservatory called Groove, where he studied classical guitar, music
            theory, and jazz guitar with Leyve Miranda and Luis Cavalcante. In this period Peter
            started going deep into the music of George Benson and Wes Montgomery.

            Also in this period, Peter studied Brazilian music with the extraordinary guitar player Lula
            Galvão, and also piano with the great pianist and arranger Edmundo Cassis, who gave him
            a new vision about harmony.

            After many years of studying day and night, Peter started giving his first lessons and re-
            alized he had a great talent to teach and encourage others. Since then teaching has also
            become his passion.

            It did not take long until musicians all over the world started keeping their eyes on him
            because of his ability, technique and musicality. He was very advanced for an 18 year old
            boy. As a matter of fact, Peter was known in the musical scene as a prodigy since he was 14.

            Sometime later Peter was introduced to his idol and mentor George Benson. The connec-
            tion between them was instantaneous. The great Benson was amazed at Peter’s talent and
            how he had decoded many of his secrets. After a few months of friendship, Peter became
                                    68
            Mr. Benson’s private student and it has continued for the past 15 years.

            During the last six years Peter Farrell and George Benson have been dedicated to this col-
                           46
            lection of method books that they have created together: The George Benson Method.

            Peter Farrell has recorded and played with many great artists all over the world such as Jane
            Monheit, Brian McKnight, Eumir Deodato, Cesar Camargo Mariano, Romero Lubambo,
            Adam Rogers, and Didier Lockwood just to name a few.

                                        QUOTES
     “I am glad that I met Peter Farrell because he has been my very good friend for many years and I trust him.
     There is no man on earth that knows more about my playing than this man, and also I just want you to know,
     there’s no place else out there you’re going to get this type of information with this kind of depth”.

     George Benson



     “I come to you with my observations about this monumental work: the George Benson Method books.
     I’ve had an opportunity to look some of them over. I find them to be extremely thorough, concise, well
     laid out and easy to understand. They would be a tremendous asset to anyone who works with them.
     That includes students, instructors, amateurs and professionals alike. These method books will take their
     place in the jazz and improvisational musical educational field and will be one of the most sought after
     volumes of any that I’ve seen thus far. I think Peter’s and GB’s work will be used for years as a valuable
     reference for education in improvisatory music and jazz guitar in particular.”

     Andy LaVerne

     musical and organizational sense of Peter Farell, we can say that there is no such rich, detailed and for-

                     Filipe Faria                  Bill Moio                      Peter Farrell


             F
        I met ilipe        F aria when I became his music theory teacher in a jazz conservatory many
        years ago. After 10 years of friendship, he became the bass player for my band “The Peter Farrell
        Group”.


                                    68
        However, life took us onto different paths. I had to quit my own band project to start organizing
        what would be the most important project of my life: The George Benson Method in collaboration
        with GB.

                           46
        George and I worked very well together while we were writing everything on paper, but we real-
        ized that we would need someone to help us input and format all of this music into the computer.
        This would be too time consuming for us because we were totally focused on the concepts, ideas
        and subjects for the method.

        During this period, one day out of the blue Filipe called me and asked if I would like to put the
        “The Peter Farrell Group” back on the road because we had so much fun playing together. That
        phone call was a Godsend. Until that moment I had forgotten that Filipe was my best music theory
        student and not just that, he was the most organized guy I had ever met, and I trusted him more
        than anyone. So, needing someone to fill that role, Filipe fit like a glove for GB and me. So we
        decided to put him in this project with us.

        I can honestly say that without Filipe Faria, GB and I would have been in trouble. Nobody could
        have been a better friend and would have done the incredible work he has done. GB and I will be
        thankful for you always and forever. Love you brotha !!!
                                2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved


the George Benson Method                                                                                   4668




                 B
            I met ill      M   oio at a “jam session” organized by the great engineer and musician
            Mark Decozio . It happened in Phoenix in 2018 during one of my trips with Filipe to meet
            George Benson at his home in Arizona. The connection between Bill and me was instan-
            taneous and it seemed that we had been friends forever. I can say that Bill Moio is not just
            an amazing guitar player, but one of the coolest guys I have ever met. If you like jazz and
            you don’t know him, you don’t know what you are missing !!!

            It is very important to mention that during this period I was looking for someone to correct
            the English written in the GBM books. Because I am not a native speaker and during my
            lessons with GB I had to write the information he was giving me down very fast, I know
            there would probably be many mistakes.

            Some days after the “jam” Bill invited Filipe and me over to his place and see his amazing
            guitar collection. When we got there, he also showed us hundreds of GB transcriptions,
            and I noticed that they were extremely well done and super organized. So I looked to
            Filipe and we smiled at each other as if to say: we have found the guy.

            I immediately called GB telling him that I thought we had found another special guy to help
            us with the GBM books project. GB asked to meet him first, so we went to a restaurant and

                                   68
            had lunch together. We talked a lot and had a lot of fun together, and just like Filipe and
            me , GB was very confident about Bill as the new member for the project. So finally we had
            the complete team.

                           46
            One day after that meeting we sent Bill the collection of books to start being edited, and
            again I can honestly say that GB and I were very lucky for the second time. Besides the fact
            that Bill had the patience to read 5000 pages and correct all of them, he also corrected
            the music notation. So I have no words to say how much I like this guy. I wish there were
            more guys like Bill Moio, the world would be a much better place. GB, Filipe and I will be
            thankful for you always and forever.

            Love you brotha !!!

                                                                                                       4668




How to use this book
Although a big part of this method is exclusively dedicated to the guitar, (because it refers to
chord construction and basic visualization of the guitar fretboard),it was created as a tool for all
instruments of all levels (beginner, intermediate and advanced). However, even if you are an
advanced player, make sure you know start at the beginning of the book, otherwise you will
easily get lost by the end of the book.


This method should be used as a dictionary of ideas and techniques. It's important to understand
that the way we approach the musical concepts, ideas and techniques presented here are unique
and cannot be found elsewhere.


Do not worry about learning everything from every chapter. You can go ahead as soon as you
understand the concepts. But always remember that you can come back to any chapter of any
book in order to learn more about a determined subject you want to study again.


Be careful when trying to learn too much at one time. Music is not about quantity but quality.



George and I have been working for many years on this giant collection of books to give you the
                               68
best we can and help those that love the guitar the way we do. We hope this method can open a
new world that was unknown until now. You have a treasure in your hands. It contains all the
knowledge that George Benson has been teaching me privately over the last 15 years.


Thanks a lot,
                      46
Peter Farrell

                                              Important device:
The patterns and scale visualizations in this book may have different time signatures in order to
help with the organization and visualization. Notice that depending on where and how we play
them on the fretboard, we will have more or less possibilities. Don’t let the time signatures
confuse you. Learn the ideas that you like the most and apply them where you feel they sound
the best. Give more attention to the single lines shown.

    Chapter 1                                                                                         4668




    Staff, Clefs & Musical Notes
    Intervals:
    A musical interval is the distance from one note (sound) to another. They may be described
    as perfect, major, minor, diminished or augmented. The smallest existing interval is called
    half step, which can also be called half tone or semitone; two half steps form one whole step,
    which can be called whole tone.


    Musical Notes:
    Musical notes are sounds represented by determined symbols in musical notation. There
    are 12 existing notes in western music, but actually we only need 7 of them for such
    representation. We will call them natural notes: Do, Re, Mi, Fa, Sol, La, Ti. Each one of these
    notes are represented by the 7 Jirst letters from the alphabet:

      • Do = C / Re = D / Mi = E / Fa = F / Sol = G / La = A / Ti = B

    The other 5 notes will be derived from these main 7, using what we call music accidentals,
    which can rise or lower each note one half step.
